data.name: Mahmud Abunada
data.description: At over 1.90m (6ft 3in), the Al-Rayyan No 1 is one of Asia’s tallest goalkeepers. But while he has impressed with his consistency in the Qatar Stars League over the years, his international career has been an unusual one. The Doha-born shot-stopper was first called up way back in 2020 and while he was in and out of the squad over the next five years – and sometimes in action with the under-23 team – he never made it off the bench. When the call finally came it was in the two World Cup playoffs last October, the games that clinched Qatar’s first ever qualification. In some of the biggest games in the country’s history, he stood firm.

data.name: Pedro Miguel
data.description: Also known as Ro-Ro, the defender could, had history gone differently, be turning out for Cape Verde this summer as he represented the African country in age-group matches. Born in Portugal, spending years with Benfica at youth level without ever making a first-team appearance, he can play as right-back or in the middle of defence or even in midfield if necessary. Was a star at the 2019 Asian Cup and there were high expectations going into 2022 that were not quite met. Has since broken the century barrier in appearances for Qatar.

data.name: Lucas Mendes
data.description: For a while it looked as if this left-footed Brazilian-born centre-back was going to be the hero of qualification with a winning goal against Uzbekistan in the 12th minute of injury time, controlling a header in a crowded area to fire into the top corner. In the end, Qatar still needed the playoffs to qualify but here was a player for the big occasion. In 2014, he left Marseille to join El-Jaish and, although nobody really thought that he would be there for nine years and be naturalised in 2023 as a Qatari citizen, that is exactly what happened. He could have been useful a few months earlier at the previous World Cup, and he will look to make up for lost time in June and July.

data.name: Issa Laye
data.description: The Senegal-born defender received Qatari citizenship in 2025 after five years on the books at second-tier Lusail. At 1.88m (6ft 2in), he brings height and power into the backline, though can also play in midfield if needed. Given that Qatar’s defence has looked vulnerable and that making the most of set pieces is seen as crucial by the coaching staff, Laye could have a significant part to play in North America.

data.name: Jassem Gaber
data.description: An energetic defensive midfielder who can also play further back if needed, Gaber offers industry and intelligence in his position. He does not get forward too much – his only international goal came via a hefty deflection in the semi-final of the 2023 Asian Cup – and his work goes more unnoticed than most, by fans at least. The coaching staff knows the player’s worth, though. “He is the perfect team player, he does the simple things very well and opponents don’t enjoy facing him as they know it will be a difficult 90 minutes,” said the captain, Hassan Al-Haydos, after the Asian Cup triumph. At 24, the World Cup could come at a perfect time to make an international name for himself.

data.name: Abdulaziz Hatem
data.description: Made his debut even before Sepp Blatter announced that Qatar had won the bidding for the 2022 World Cup, and is still going strong, though is not quite the fixture he once was in the centre of midfield, not surprising given that he is closer to 40 than 30. Still has a reputation for scoring important goals – such as the winner in the quarter-final in the 2019 Asian Cup and then another goal in the final – and spectacular ones too. More often on display are his excellent close-control skills, especially as he receives the ball from the defence. Won’t expect too many minutes in the United States.

data.name: Ahmed Alaaeldin
data.description: Has a good leap – just look at his towering header in the Arab Cup against Syria – and never stops buzzing around the frontline. Moved to Doha from Egypt at the age of 10 with his engineer father, a typical Qatari story. Less common is that he made his international debut at the age of 20 way back in 2013. He now has close to 70 appearances, but has never quite established himself as the main man in attack for the national team and has been in and out of the side for 13 years.

data.name: Edmilson Junior
data.description: Eligible to represent the country of his birth, Belgium – where he played seven seasons for Sint-Truidense and Standard Liège – as well as Brazil, the winger finally made the switch to Qatar in October 2024. It’s easy to see why he was in demand. Can play on the right or left and also in the middle and takes a mean free-kick too. Has an air of Son Heung-min when he is in possession on the edge of the area, curling shots into far corners or drilling balls into the bottom of the net.

data.name: Mohammed Muntari
data.description: Scored Qatar’s only goal at the 2022 World Cup and, in 2025, scored the fastest goal in the history of the Qatar Stars League at just eight seconds. Born and raised in Ghana, trained at the academy owned by Nii Lamptey, the former Aston Villa, Coventry City and Anderlecht forward and one-time "African Pelé". An aerial threat at more than 1.90m (6ft 3in), he made the switch back to don the maroon back in 2014. "We play football first because we love to play football," he explained just before the last World Cup "Second because we want to take care of the people we want to take care of." Of his switch he said: "It wasn't financially motivated. I could have been playing for Ghana and still be earning much."

data.name: Hassan Al-Haydos
data.description: Returned from 469 days of international retirement in 2025 at the request of Julen Lopetegui and it came as no surprise. “He brings a presence to the team and is a real leader and that can sometimes make the difference,” said the coach. Then there is the experience, too. Al-Haydos is Qatar’s most-capped player, with more than 180 appearances for his country and over 40 goals. Played a pivotal role in Qatar’s 2019 and 2023 Asian Cup triumphs. A one-club man who has won over 30 trophies in his career; the first league title with Al-Sadd came back in 2007. He won the Asian Champions League four years later.

data.name: Akram Afif
data.description: The country’s big star, big hope and the magician on the wing, and not just because he has been known to pull playing cards out of his socks upon scoring. If the two-time Asian player of the year plays well, then Qatar have a chance. Did not have the best World Cup in 2022, when hopes were high, and it was also disappointing that he never made it in Europe so this is his big chance to show that he is capable of mixing it with the big boys. Can dribble past defenders and create – there were 10 assists as Qatar won the 2019 Asian Cup – and score: he hit eight goals as Qatar won the 2023 Asian Cup, including a hat-trick in the final. Qatar need Afif to shine.

data.name: Karim Boudiaf
data.description: Scored one of the best goals ever seen at Crewe Alexandra’s Gresty Road, his first for Qatar, a left-footed curler from outside the area to earn a draw with Northern Ireland in 2015. “Every team needs a player like Karim Boudiaf, someone that the other players can look to when the game becomes tense or chaotic,” said the sometime Qatar coach Carlos Queiroz. “He is a calm presence.” The Algerian-born midfielder is as aggressive as he has always been as he approaches his 36th birthday. Boudiaf has more than 120 caps and a big part of his longevity is that he longer dashes around in defensive midfield to no purpose but reads the game, breaks up an attack and starts one of his own.

data.name: Ayoub Al-Oui
data.description: A late addition to the team, with Julen Lopetegui handing the youngster his international debut in October 2025, throwing him into the mix for the latter stages of qualification. Bringing some much-needed youth into the squad, he has continued to feature since and could be in line for a starting spot. It is a just reward for a fine domestic season for Al-Gharafa, where he was named the best under-23 player in the Qatar Stars League in May. Brings physicality to the right-back position, though has also played at centre-back and in defensive midfield for his club.

data.name: Homam Ahmed
data.description: Played all but seven minutes of the 2022 World Cup and still has a point to prove. At the start of the year, the rangy left-back, who can play further up the pitch, made the move to play in Spain’s second tier. It took the defender time to settle but, as the second half of the season progressed, he started to get more and more time on the pitch, which can only help get in the good books of the coach, Julen Lopetegui, who wants Ahmed to add goals to his game. If so, he could have a great World Cup.

data.name: Yusuf Abdurisag
data.description: The speedy winger was at the centre of an incident in 2023 as Qatar played New Zealand in a friendly, with the All Whites refusing to play the second half after alleging racist abuse. The day after Abdurisag said: “During last night’s game, I was the target of racist abuse from a member of the opposing team,” he said. “To my complete shock, that same player accused me of using offensive language and the game was abandoned.” Has struggled to get playing time for Qatar in recent months and is likely to be a squad player at the World Cup.

data.name: Boualem Khoukhi
data.description: An old warrior who started out as a youth player at Algeria’s Cheraga. “He outclassed both opponents and teammates. He had a good understanding of the game, which allowed him to anticipate the opposing team's attacks,” says the coach Farid Zemiti. “He was just as good on the ball as he was covering.” After starting out as a winger, has since played well over 100 games in defence and midfield, and his 21st international goal came in the playoff win over the United Arab Emirates in October that clinched a first-ever qualification. Strong in the shot and commanding in the air, a legend of Qatar whatever happens in the summer.

data.name: Ahmed Al-Ganehi
data.description: The winger, who plays with a smile on his face, has come up through the ranks of the Qatar national team to become a solid squad player. Has earned something of a reputation for being a supersub and scoring goals in big domestic games. Can play on either flank, but usually appears on the right, and is adept at running into space behind the defence. Has yet to really catch the eye for the national team since making his debut in 2024 but the World Cup could be the stage.

data.name: Sultan Al-Brake
data.description: The left-back spent time in Spain with Cultural Leonesa in the early part of his career and perhaps it was there where he perfected the kind of cross that hangs in the air just waiting for someone to get on the end of it. “Tactially it was an education,” he said later, reflecting on his European time. “It was also a chance to see a different lifestyle and how the players prepared for games.” It can’t have done his confidence any harm, either. Good on the ball, he likes to get forward, using his pace to drive at the opposition. Never quite managed to become an automatic starter for his country but has proved to be a solid back-up whenever called upon.

data.name: Almoez Ali
data.description: It’s hard to know what to make of Ali’s career. “He comes alive in the area but remains calm when there is a sight of goal and that is why he is a top-class striker,” said the then Qatar coach, Félix Sánchez, after the 2019 Asian Cup. That’s when Ali rose to fame with nine goals and he has since become Qatar’s leading goalscorer. Had early spells in Austria and Spain but has now been with Al-Duhail since 2016. At one time, the Sudan-born striker, fast and able to find space, looked set to become Asia’s number one goalscorer – but it never quite happened. Still a threat.

data.name: Ahmed Fathy
data.description: Son of an Egyptian football coach who also worked as a youth coach for Al-Sadd, Qatar’s leading club, for more than 25 years, Fathy always had a decent chance of making it. Working hard also helped and his energy as a defensive midfielder has stood him in good stead, even if he does not quite have the same engine as a few years ago. Fathy captained the national team at times, notably at the 2023 Gold Cup, and really grew into the responsibility of the role.

data.name: Salah Zakaria
data.description: Long seen as the next goalkeeper to take the gloves, but it has not happened yet. Now 27, Zakaria has established himself as Al-Duhail’s No 1 but was linked with the Egyptian giants Zamalak in 2025, partly because he has Egyptian citizenship. Not only a solid shot-stopper, Zakaria is valued by coaches for his distribution as he is equally comfortable with both feet, whether launching long or short. It is unlikely that he is going to start games at this World Cup but has shown at club level that he is a reliable back-up.

data.name: Meshaal Barsham
data.description: Brother of the 2020 Olympic high jump gold medal winner, Mutaz Barsham, Meshaal has similar agility and has made a name for penalty-saving prowess. Born in Sudan and, after the dismal opener at the 2022 World Cup, came in for the remaining two group games against Senegal and the Netherlands but still conceded five. Bounced back to be the best goalkeeper at Qatar’s 2023 Asian Cup triumph and is not short on mental fortitude. Not the tallest shot-stopper at just below six feet but has the Barsham spring and can reach shots that others can’t. “He is capable of making amazing saves,” said the sometime Qatar coach Félix Sánchez. “And he’s very professional, the kind of goalkeeper that all coaches want.”

data.name: Assim Madibo
data.description: Had an interesting start to his career with spells in France, Spain and Belgium before coming to the Qatar Stars League. The midfielder has been compared to N’Golo Kanté with his tireless running and ability to read the game. Has been in and out of the team but he did play a significant part in qualification, including every minute of the two playoff games that finally sealed the deal for the Maroons. “He did everything I asked of him, especially when the tension in the stadium was high with so much on the line,” said the Qatar coach, Julen Lopetegui. “It was an impressive performance.”

data.name: Tahsin Jamshid
data.description: Fans in India don’t have a team at the World Cup and, while many will be backing Spain, England, Brazil or Argentina, some will be cheering on Jamshid. The teenager’s parents came to Qatar from football-loving Kerala in India and the winger, born during the World Cup in 2006, is unfazed by the challenge. “For me, Qatar is my own country. I'm a Qatari citizen. Having already played for the under-17 and under-19 teams, this selection didn't come as a surprise," he said.  Quick with an appetite for running at defenders, Jamshid, who plays on the left but can do a job on the right, looks to have a bright future.

data.name: Al-Hashmi Al-Hussain
data.description: No stranger to controversy, Hussein managed to upset the nation of India during the qualifiers. With Qatar losing 1-0 in a qualifier, he pounced on a ball that had looked to have clearly gone out of play, passing for a teammate to score and putting a huge dent in the dreams of Indian fans. The All India Football Federation president, Kalyan Chaubey, asked for a “thorough investigation” into the “grave error”. The AIFF added in a statement: “Furthermore, we have urged them [the AFC head of referees] to explore the possibilities of sporting compensation to address the injustice.” Nothing came of it in the end, though. Hussein is a rarity in Qatari circles as he has spent time playing in Europe, the lower leagues of Spain to be exact, and has recently returned to the national team.
